event: Neolithic farmers establish agricultural settlements in the fertile lowlands of present-day Slovakia.
Bronze Age cultures such as the Únětice and later hillfort communities flourish across the region.
Celtic tribes settle the territory, founding fortified oppida and minting the silver Biatec coins near Bratislava.
Roman influence extends to the Danube frontier as Germanic and Celtic peoples interact with the empire.
Roman soldiers inscribe a victory at Laugaricio (modern Trenčín), the northernmost Roman epigraphic record in the region.
Germanic Quadi and other tribes dominate the area during the upheavals of the Migration Period.
Slavic peoples migrate into the region, becoming the dominant population of the territory.
Slavic tribes join Samo's Empire, the first known political union of Western Slavs against Avar domination.
Prince Mojmír I unites the Moravian and Nitrian principalities to form Great Moravia.
Byzantine missionaries Cyril and Methodius arrive in Great Moravia, introducing Slavonic liturgy and the Glagolitic script.
Great Moravia collapses after Magyar invasions, opening the way for Hungarian control of the region.
The territory of Slovakia is gradually incorporated into the emerging Kingdom of Hungary.
The Mongol invasion devastates the region, prompting King Béla IV to build stone castles for defense.
King Matthias Corvinus founds the Universitas Istropolitana in Bratislava, the first university in the territory.
After the Ottoman victory at Mohács, the region passes to Habsburg rule as part of Royal Hungary.
Bratislava (Pressburg) becomes the capital and coronation city of Royal Hungary as Ottomans occupy the south.
Archbishop Péter Pázmány founds the University of Trnava, a key center of the Counter-Reformation.
Maria Theresa is crowned in Bratislava, ushering in Habsburg reforms across the Hungarian lands.
Anton Bernolák codifies the first standard of written Slovak, advancing the national revival.
Ľudovít Štúr establishes the modern Slovak literary language based on central Slovak dialects.
Slovaks issue the Demands of the Slovak Nation during the revolutions sweeping the Habsburg Empire.
The cultural institution Matica slovenská is founded in Martin to promote Slovak national identity.
Hungarian authorities close the Matica slovenská and intensify Magyarization policies against Slovaks.
The Martin Declaration unites Slovaks with Czechs in the new state of Czechoslovakia after World War I.
Comenius University is founded in Bratislava as the first modern Slovak-language higher institution.
Slovakia gains autonomy within Czechoslovakia following the Munich Agreement and the First Vienna Award.
The clerical-authoritarian Slovak State declares independence under Jozef Tiso as a Nazi German client.
The Slovak National Uprising rises against Nazi occupation and the collaborationist regime.
Czechoslovakia is restored as Soviet and other Allied forces liberate Slovakia at the end of World War II.
The Communist Party seizes full power in Czechoslovakia, establishing a one-party state.
The Prague Spring reforms are crushed by a Warsaw Pact invasion in August.
Czechoslovakia is reorganized as a federation, creating a distinct Slovak Socialist Republic.
The Velvet Revolution peacefully ends Communist rule in Czechoslovakia.
Slovak and Czech leaders agree to dissolve the federation in the so-called Velvet Divorce.
The independent Slovak Republic is established on 1 January and joins the United Nations.
A reformist coalition under Mikuláš Dzurinda wins elections, steering Slovakia toward Western integration.
Rudolf Schuster becomes the first president elected directly by Slovak voters.
Slovakia joins both NATO and the European Union, completing its Euro-Atlantic integration.
Slovakia enters the Schengen Area, abolishing border controls with neighboring EU states.
Slovakia adopts the euro as its national currency, replacing the Slovak koruna.
The murder of journalist Ján Kuciak sparks mass protests and the resignation of the government.
Zuzana Čaputová is elected as Slovakia's first female president.
Robert Fico returns to power as prime minister after parliamentary elections.
Prime Minister Robert Fico survives an assassination attempt, shocking the nation.
